content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Part 4 of a series exploring what AI Native really means for organisations, governance and the future of work.
Every organisation worries about losing knowledge.
Very few worry about keeping it for too long and that’s kind of understandable.
It’s like that box of 20 year old cables and phone chargers you have somewhere just in-case it’s useful again in the future.
For decades we’ve invested in document management systems, knowledge bases and wikis because institutional knowledge disappears surprisingly quickly. People leave, projects finish and teams reorganise. Capturing what the organisation knows has always seemed like an obvious goal.
Now, AI Native quietly changes the question.
Now, perhaps the challenge isn’t preserving knowledge it’s knowing when to stop trusting or using it.
We Already Understand This
Every business already manages information with an expiry date.
Price lists are versioned.
Policies are reviewed.
Product documentation is updated and replaced.
Technical standards evolve over time.
Nobody expects a five-year-old pricing guide to remain authoritative simply because it’s still available.
The latest version becomes the truth.
The older versions remain part of the history and that’s fine, we understand this instinctively.
Yet conversations rarely work that way.
Conversations Don’t Have Versions
A Slack message written six months ago doesn’t suddenly expire.
Neither does a Teams conversation. a meeting summary or an informal recommendation.
They just simply continue to exist…
Now imagine those conversations forming part of an AI Native platform’s understanding of your organisation.
Not because they’re official, just simply because they happened.
That’s a very different model of organisational memory.
Organisations Drift
One of the first lessons that you learn in technology is that systems drift.
Configurations change, threats evolve and business priorities move.
The same thing happens to organisations.

• Suppliers improve.

• Departments reorganise.

• Processes mature.

• Products evolve.

• And people develop.
The organisation you have today is almost certainly not the organisation you had eighteen months ago.
Yet AI Native may still be drawing confidence from conversations that belonged to a business that no longer exists.
Yesterday’s Truth
Imagine your procurement team spends months complaining about a suppliers poor service, late deliveries and constant need for escalation.
The conversations and evidence is everywhere.
But then the supplier is acquired and new management arrives. The service improves dramatically and within weeks, nobody complains anymore.
Not because people have forgotten, but because there’s nothing left to complain about.
Six months later someone asks the AI:
*”Which supplier should we avoid?”*
The answer may still be influenced by hundreds of historic conversations, and their weight in numbers, describing a supplier that no longer exists.
The AI hasn’t made a mistake, it’s remembered faithfully.
The Half-Life Of Knowledge
Perhaps organisational knowledge has something remarkably similar to a half-life.
Some knowledge remains valid for years.
Some becomes questionable after months.
Some should disappear almost immediately.
The challenge isn’t collecting organisational memory.
It’s understanding how quickly different types of knowledge lose their value.
A technical standard might survive for five years.
A project lesson perhaps two.
An opinion expressed during a difficult meeting?
Possibly only until the next coffee break.
Memory Needs Governance
We’ve spent years applying governance to documents.

• Version control.

• Review dates.

• Approval workflows.

• Retention periods.
Perhaps AI Native requires exactly the same discipline for organisational memory.
Not because the AI forgets too easily, but because it doesn’t forget easily enough.
Some knowledge should be reviewed.
Some should be revalidated.
Some should quietly expire.
Otherwise yesterday’s assumptions can slowly become tomorrow’s recommendations.
The Hidden Risk
One of the biggest risks in AI Native won’t be hallucination.
It will be confidence.
Confidence built upon information that was perfectly accurate…
…when it was written.
Businesses change.
Markets change.
People change.
Knowledge changes with them.
The AI doesn’t automatically know that.
Somebody has to teach it.
The Real Shift
We’ve spent decades asking how to preserve organisational knowledge.
AI Native introduces a different question.
How do we stop yesterday quietly becoming today’s truth?
Because memory isn’t valuable simply because it’s permanent.
It’s valuable because it’s still relevant.
This article was developed with the assistance of AI to help refine tone and structure, but the core ideas, personal insights, and final edits are my own.
